notes: 1-6. The Pashur in this passage is not to be confused with the Pashur of 21:1 or 38:1--the two men are of different lineages. The Lord set Jeremiah as prophet over the nations of the earth; and Pashur was appointed as overseer of the temple by an earthly tribunal. Now the earthly overseer asserted his power over the heavenly overseer. The Lord undoubtedly allowed Pashur to do this so he would bring judgments upon himself. One evidence of this is the Lord's personal prophecy against Pashur after he beat Jeremiah and placed him in the stocks overnight. Also, the Lord's changing of Pashur's name symbolizes the fate which he was to suffer. Pashur's new name, Magor-missabib, means "Fear round about" (King James Version of the Bible marginal note) or "Terror all around" (Anchor Bible): The judgments which the Lord pronounced upon Pashur through Jeremiah would bring fear or terror not only to him, but to all his family and friends. (Monte S. Nyman, The Words of Jeremiah, p.64)

probably equivalent to the Presiding Bishop. The Hebrew word paqid, which we have already seen translated as "bishop" by Clement, is translated respectively "governor" and "officer" in Jeremiah 20:1 and 29:25-26 in the English; but it is rendered "inspector" in the French Louis Segond version. It therefore apparently applies to the High Priest of Israel, the Presiding Bishop. The title "inspector" was also used by the early Christians (John A. Tvedtnes, The Church of the Old Testament, p.77-78)
The inspector seems to fit the description of a bishop, and therefore the inspector for "all the camps" is

terror . . . to all thy friends--who have believed thy false promises (Jer 20:6). The sense must be in order to accord with "fear round about" (Jer 20:3). I will bring terror on thee and on all thy friends, that terror arising from thyself, namely, thy false prophecies. Thou and thy prophecies will be seen, to the dismay both of thee and thy dupes, to have caused their ruin and thine. MAURER'S translation is therefore not needed, "I will give up thee and all thy friends to terror." (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

7-13 Jeremiah was so moved upon by the Lord that he swallowed his human pride and proclaimed the Lord's message, as the Spirit now superseded the reasoning or decisions of man. He was reluctant to prophesy because he feared for his life, but, knowing the Lord's desire, he proceeded and placed his trust in the Lord's protection. It is possible that he was aware of the principle that one's life is not to be taken before his mission is completed (see Mosiah 13:1-5; Joseph Smith, Teachings of the Prophet Joseph Smith, sel. Joseph Fielding Smith (Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Company, 1938), pp. 274, 328). (Monte S. Nyman, The Words of Jeremiah, p.64)

deceived--Others translate as Margin, "Thou hast enticed" or "persuaded me," namely, to undertake the prophetic office, "and I was persuaded," that is, suffered myself to be persuaded to undertake what I find too hard for me. So the Hebrew word is used in a good sense (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

Even the most faithful among us will have moments of loneliness and rejection. From Liberty Jail, Joseph Smith cried out, "O God, where art thou? And where is the pavilion that covereth thy hiding place?" (D&C 121:1.) Soon afterward, the voice of the Spirit whispered to Joseph: "Know thou, my son, that all these things shall give thee experience, and shall be for thy good. The Son of Man hath descended below them all. Art thou greater than he?" (D&C 122:7-8.) Jeremiah had a similar struggle: "O Lord, . . . thou art stronger than I, and hast prevailed. . . . Then I said, I will not make mention of him, nor speak any more in his name. But his word was in mine heart as a burning fire shut up in my bones. . . . But the Lord is with me as a mighty terrible one." (Jeremiah 20:7-9, 11.) And even the Savior, in his hour of greatest darkness and greatest triumph, pleaded, "If thou be willing, remove this cup from me: nevertheless not my will, but thine, be done." (Luke 22:42.) (Bruce C. Hafen, The Broken Heart: Applying the Atonement to Life's Experiences, p.56)

Our "how long" questions, honestly asked, can help us to increase our patience with God's timing. Prophets have been involved in similar expressions and experiences in the midst of their deep anguish and puzzlement (see Jeremiah 20:9). (Neal A. Maxwell, That Ye May Believe, p.157)

is also a metaphor used to describe the witness of the Spirit that is associated with the receipt of the Holy Ghost. (See Jeremiah 20:9.) (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Robert L. Millet, Doctrinal Commentary on the Book of Mormon, 4 vols., 1:, p.364)

Baptism of fire and of the Holy Ghost] Fire is an agent of purification. When a person is baptized by fire the dross of sin is burned from his soul and he thus becomes a fit abiding place for the Holy Ghost. Fire

familiars--[‘enowsh shalom] literally, "men of my peace"; those who pretended to be on peaceable terms with me (Ps 41:9). Jeremiah is a type of Messiah, referred to in that Psalm. (See Jer 38:22; Job 19:19; Ps 55:13, 14; Lu 11:53, 54). (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)
reins (kilyah) kidneys of seat of emotion and affection (fig.)
heart (leb) inner man, mind, will, heart, understanding

14-18. This scripture is about one of the lowest points of Jeremiah's life. His depression might be better understood by reflecting upon a parallel situation related in the Book of Mormon: Mormon's mission among the destruction-bound Nephites. Writing of his circumstances, Mormon recorded: "a continual scene of wickedness and abominations has been before mine eyes ever since I have been sufficient to behold the ways of man" (Mormon 2:18; see also 5:8-9). Jeremiah's surroundings unquestionably paralleled those of Mormon's, for the conditions are always such in societies ripe for destruction. (Monte S. Nyman, The Words of Jeremiah, p.64)
A man child--The birth of a son is in the East a special subject of joy; whereas that of a daughter is often not so. (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)
